figures.gloss: The Sufi order as institution: a master (shaykh, pir) binding disciples through initiation and an unbroken chain of transmission (silsila) reaching back to the Prophet — Qadiri, Naqshbandi, Chishti, Mevlevi and hundreds more.
figures.facet: Mysticism given a genealogy: ecstatic experience is not free-floating but franchised through lineage, so that a disciple in Delhi or Konya can name every hand between his own and Muhammad's.
figures.provenance: Order histories from al-Jilani (d. 1166, Qadiriyya), Mu'in al-Din Chishti (d. 1236), Baha' al-Din Naqshband (d. 1389); surveyed in Trimingham, The Sufi Orders in Islam, and Schimmel, Mystical Dimensions of Islam.

figures.gloss: The rhythmic repetition of God's Name — voiced or silent, breath-tied, sometimes danced — practiced until remembrance displaces the rememberer; the Mevlevi whirling sema is its most famous choreography.
figures.facet: The technology at Sufism's core: not doctrine but a repeatable practice for dismantling the self with a single word, tuned differently by every order (the Naqshbandi silent, the Rifa'i loud).
figures.provenance: Qur'anic injunction to remember God often (e.g., 33:41) as the proof-text; manuals such as al-Qushayri's Risala and Schimmel's treatment of dhikr technique.

figures.gloss: The ecstatic of Baghdad executed in 922 after years of imprisonment, remembered above all for the utterance 'Ana al-Haqq' — 'I am the Truth/Real' — divine union spoken aloud in the marketplace.
figures.facet: The tradition's boundary-stone: later Sufis (Junayd's sober school) judged him not wrong but INDISCREET — the secret of union was true and should have stayed secret; his gallows became a station of the path.
figures.provenance: Louis Massignon, The Passion of al-Hallaj; the trial and execution are historically documented, while the exact circumstances of the 'Ana al-Haqq' utterance rest on later hagiography — flagged.

figures.gloss: The Andalusian 'Greatest Master' (al-Shaykh al-Akbar, 1165-1240), whose Meccan Revelations and Ringstones of Wisdom articulate the vision later named wahdat al-wujud — the oneness of being, all existence as the self-disclosure of the One.
figures.facet: The metaphysical ceiling of the tradition: every thing that exists is a face God shows Himself — though the label wahdat al-wujud is his school's coinage more than his own phrase, a precision his defenders and detractors both blur.
figures.provenance: Al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya and Fusus al-Hikam; Chittick, The Sufi Path of Knowledge, including the point that the term wahdat al-wujud is scarcely Ibn 'Arabi's own.

figures.gloss: The Persian poet of Konya (1207-1273) whose meeting with the wandering dervish Shams of Tabriz turned a jurist into the singer of the Masnavi — six books of couplets his followers call 'the Qur'an in Persian' — and whose circle became the Mevlevi order.
figures.facet: Proof that a mystical tradition can conquer through beauty alone: Rumi's verse carried Sufism further than any conquest or mission, into languages and centuries that never saw a lodge.
figures.provenance: Masnavi-yi Ma'navi and Divan-i Shams; Franklin Lewis, Rumi: Past and Present, East and West. The Mevlevi order was institutionalized by his son Sultan Walad.

figures.gloss: The saint whose closeness to God persists after death, sought at tomb-shrines from Ajmer to Touba through visitation (ziyara), offerings, and petitions for intercession — the devotional infrastructure of much of the Islamic world.
figures.facet: Where Sufism meets the ground: the shrine network made the friends of God locally addressable — and made Sufism the prime target of the modern Salafi campaign against 'grave-worship,' from 19th-century polemic to the demolition of shrines in our own decade.
figures.provenance: Classical sainthood doctrine in al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi and Ibn 'Arabi; shrine culture and its Salafi opponents documented across modern ethnography and reportage (e.g., destruction of Sufi shrines in Timbuktu, 2012, tried before the ICC).

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: Every silsila terminates in the Prophet (most via 'Ali) — Sufism is Islam's interior discipline claiming direct descent, not a separate religion beside it.
GOLD SEAM: Fana, annihilation in the Beloved, is the terminus of dhikr practice — the parent shard already carries Sufism's crown concept; one seam, same body.

GOLD SEAM: The Alevi cem's station of judgment is named dar-i Mansur, 'the gallows of Mansur' — al-Hallaj's execution ritually re-lived in Anatolia, a documented borrowing of the martyr into the liturgy.
GOLD SEAM: Haci Bektas was a Khorasani Sufi pir and the Bektashi order bearing his name is itself a tariqa — Alevism grows from Sufi rootstock, though the degree of continuity is debated.

GOLD SEAM: The Guru Granth Sahib canonizes verses attributed to the Chishti master Shaykh Farid — a Sufi voice bound inside Sikh scripture; the inclusion is fact, though whether the hymns are Farid's own or his Pakpattan successors' is debated.
GOLD SEAM: Mevlevi sema and Alevi semah — two ecstatic turning rites of the same Anatolian soil and era; kinship of milieu and vocabulary, not proven descent.

GOLD SEAM: Breath-tied repetition of the divine Name until the heart prays itself — the Jesus Prayer and dhikr are the classic comparanda of contemplative technique; mutual influence often suggested, never proven.

dossier.essence: The mystical-ascetic dimension of Islam, practiced within both Sunni and Shi'i communities from West Africa to Southeast Asia and now globally. It seeks direct experiential knowledge of God through purification of the self, remembrance, and love, and since the 12th-13th centuries has been organized into initiatory orders (tariqas) whose chains of transmission (silsila) reach back to the Prophet Muhammad.
dossier.cosmos: Sufism keeps the standard Islamic cosmology — one God, creation, prophecy, judgment, paradise and hell — but reads it inwardly: the world is the theater of God's self-disclosure, and the soul's real journey is a path of stations and states culminating in annihilation of the ego (fana) and subsistence in God (baqa), tasted before death rather than only after it. In the school of Ibn 'Arabi this vision reaches its metaphysical ceiling: all existence as the self-disclosure of the One, a doctrine his followers named wahdat al-wujud.
dossier.practice: The disciple binds to a master (shaykh, pir) through initiation and practices dhikr — voiced, silent, breath-tied, or danced repetition of God's Name, most famously choreographed in the Mevlevi whirling sema — alongside the ordinary obligations of Islamic law. Retreats, poetry and ritual audition (sama), and visitation (ziyara) of saints' tomb-shrines fill out the devotional life; orders such as the Qadiri, Naqshbandi, Chishti, and Mevlevi span continents, and the shrine network has made Sufism the prime target of modern Salafi polemic and iconoclasm.
dossier.canon: The Qur'an and hadith read esoterically are the bedrock, elaborated in a classical manual literature (al-Qushayri's Risala, al-Ghazali's Ihya' 'Ulum al-Din) and in monuments such as Ibn 'Arabi's al-Futuhat al-Makkiyya and Rumi's Masnavi.
dossier.reflects: The interiorization of a law-religion — the insistence that revelation exists to be tasted, engineered through repeatable technique and franchised through lineage rather than left to spontaneous grace.
sources: J. Spencer Trimingham, 'The Sufi Orders in Islam' (1971)
William C. Chittick, 'The Sufi Path of Knowledge' (1989)

links.cite: J. Spencer Trimingham, 'The Sufi Orders in Islam' (1971)
William C. Chittick, 'The Sufi Path of Knowledge' (1989)
